text: The patient is positioned in a supine lithotomy position, with legs elevated and supported, preparing for the robotic right inguinal hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure using a trocar setup typical for laparoscopic transabdominal preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair, positioning the trocars for optimal access to the surgical site.

The surgeon places two 8mm trocars in the right and left mid quadrants, centered by a 12mm trocar for the camera, as part of the robotic right inguinal hernia repair using the transabdominal preperitoneal approach.
The robotic surgical system is docked between the patient's legs, positioned for a transabdominal preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair.
The internal surface anatomy is visualized, showing the medial umbilical ligament, inferior epigastric vessels, spermatic vessels, and the vas deferens.
The surgical view reveals a pantaloon-type hernia, characterized by both direct and indirect hernia components visible within the operative field.
The surgeon begins the dissection by creating a peritoneal flap, using the established trocar setup for laparoscopic transabdominal preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair.

The surgical procedure begins medially at the level of the medial umbilical ligament, with visible anatomical landmarks including the medial umbilical ligament, inferior epigastric vessels, spermatic vessels, and vas deferens.
The surgeon extends the peritoneal flap laterally towards the anterior superior iliac spine, revealing underlying anatomical structures as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap, carefully staying dorsal to the transversalis fascia to ensure dissection within an avascular plane.
An arrow is used to delineate the iliotibial tract, highlighting its location in relation to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgical view reveals the genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve, identifiable within the dissected area.
The surgeon exposes Cooper's ligament medial to the hernia sac, revealing its anatomical position in relation to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon exposes the coronamortis overlying Cooper's ligament, carefully avoiding inadvertent injury to the TAC.
With Cooper's ligament fully exposed, the surgeon begins dissecting the hernia sac, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac from the deep inguinal ring and proceeds to skeletonize the spermatic cord, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs sharp and blunt dissection around the hernia sac and adjacent anatomical structures, utilizing precise techniques to separate tissue layers.
The surgeon carefully preserves the testicular vessels, visible to the right of the scissors, ensuring they remain intact along their length during the procedure.
The surgeon uses scissors to delineate the edge of the reduced peritoneal sac, highlighting its boundary within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissecting the peritoneum from its retroperitoneal structures, preparing the area for the placement of a wide sheet of mesh.
The surgeon places tacks to secure the mesh medially on Cooper's ligament and laterally above the iliopubic tract, carefully avoiding the triangle of doom and the triangle of pain.
The assistant deploys the mesh into the inguinal space through the undocked eight millimeter trocar positioned ipsilateral to the hernia defect.
The surgeon places tacks on Cooper's ligament, above Cooper's ligament anteriorly, and in the supralateral position to secure the mesh in the inguinal space.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using an attacking device, securing the flap over the previously placed mesh in the inguinal space.
